title: Grammar & Punctuation
objective: Students will explore grammar & punctuation concepts including Modal Verbs and Possibility, Progressive and Continuous Tenses, Linking paragraphs with adverbials and more.

steps.content: Have you ever thought about Modal Verbs and Possibility? Let's find out together!
Understand and use modal verbs (can, may, must, might, shall, will, could, should, would) and modal adverbs (perhaps, surely, certainly) to indicate degrees of possibility, necessity, and permission
Let's practice together! Select a modal verb to express certainty, probability, or possibility in a sentence, e.g. 'It will rain' vs 'It might rain' vs 'It could rain'

When you writes, do they choose between words like "must", "might", "could", and "should" to show whether something is certain, possible, or just an idea — rather than treating every action as definite?

Great job! Today we learned about Modal Verbs and Possibility. Select a modal verb to express certainty, probability, or possibility in a sentence, e.g. 'It will rain' vs 'It might rain' vs 'It could rain' Replace a modal verb with a modal adverb to achieve a similar effect, e.g. 'He might come' → 'Perhaps he will come'
Today we're going to learn about Progressive and Continuous Tenses. Get ready for an adventure!

Form and use the progressive (continuous) verb tenses — past progressive (was walking), present progressive (am walking), and future progressive (will be walking) — to convey ongoing actions at different times
Let me show you something cool. Write sentences using past progressive to describe an action that was ongoing at a particular time, e.g. 'She was reading when the phone rang'
If you wants to describe an action that was happening over a period of time in the past — like "I was reading when the phone rang" — can they write the verb in the right ongoing-past form?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Progressive and Continuous Tenses. Write sentences using past progressive to describe an action that was ongoing at a particular time, e.g. 'She was reading when the phone rang' Distinguish present progressive from simple present, e.g. 'I am eating lunch' (right now) vs 'I eat lunch at noon' (habitual)
Let's explore Linking paragraphs with adverbials! Have you ever wondered about this?

Link ideas across paragraphs using adverbials of time (later, meanwhile, after a while), place (nearby, far away, beyond the wall), and number (secondly, finally, in addition) to guide the reader through a multi-paragraph text

Let's practice together! Begin a new paragraph with an adverbial of time to signal a shift in time or sequence, e.g. 'Meanwhile, back at the castle...' or 'Several hours later, the storm finally passed'

When you writes a multi-paragraph story or report, do they use linking phrases — like "Meanwhile, far away…" or "By the following morning…" — to guide the reader smoothly from one section to the next?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Linking paragraphs with adverbials. Begin a new paragraph with an adverbial of time to signal a shift in time or sequence, e.g. 'Meanwhile, back at the castle...' or 'Several hours later, the storm finally passed' Use adverbials of place to move the reader to a new location between paragraphs, e.g. 'Beyond the forest, the land was flat and dry'
Have you ever thought about Cohesion within paragraphs? Let's find out together!
Use cohesive devices within a paragraph — including pronouns, adverbials (then, after that, firstly), and synonyms — to link sentences and build a coherent flow of ideas
Time to try this out: Replace repeated nouns with pronouns or synonyms to maintain cohesion without ambiguity, e.g. 'The explorer found a cave. She examined it carefully'
When you writes a paragraph, do the sentences flow together smoothly — using words like "however", "after this", or "in contrast" to link ideas, rather than every sentence starting with "And then"?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Cohesion within paragraphs: Replace repeated nouns with pronouns or synonyms to maintain cohesion without ambiguity, e.g. 'The explorer found a cave. She examined it carefully' Use adverbials of sequence (firstly, then, next, finally) and cause (therefore, as a result, consequently) to connect ideas within a paragraph

lessons.title: Grammar Terms: Modal Verbs and Clauses

steps.content: Have you ever thought about Grammar Terms: Modal Verbs and Clauses? Let's find out together!
Know and use Year 5 grammar terminology accurately when discussing reading and writing: modal verb, relative pronoun, relative clause, parenthesis, bracket, dash, cohesion, ambiguity
Let's practice together! Label a relative clause in a sentence and identify the relative pronoun that introduces it, using correct terminology
If you ask you what a "modal verb" or a "relative clause" is, can they give you a clear explanation and point to an example in a sentence?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Grammar Terms: Modal Verbs and Clauses. Label a relative clause in a sentence and identify the relative pronoun that introduces it, using correct terminology Explain why a pair of brackets or dashes creates a parenthesis and describe its function using the term 'parenthesis'
Have you ever thought about Expanded noun phrases (age 9+)? Let's find out together!
Form and use prepositional phrases (preposition + noun phrase) to add detail about time, location, or direction within sentences, recognising how they function as adjective or adverb phrases
Time to try this out: Identify the preposition and noun phrase within a prepositional phrase, e.g. in 'under the old bridge', 'under' is the preposition and 'the old bridge' is the noun phrase
When you writes directions or descriptions of a place — like "behind the old tree" or "near the top of the hill" — do they use prepositional phrases naturally to show where or when something is?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Expanded noun phrases (age 9+). Identify the preposition and noun phrase within a prepositional phrase, e.g. in 'under the old bridge', 'under' is the preposition and 'the old bridge' is the noun phrase Add prepositional phrases to expand sentences with detail about where, when, or how, e.g. 'The cat slept' → 'The cat slept on the warm windowsill throughout the afternoon'
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Brackets and dashes for parenthesis!
Use brackets, dashes, and commas to indicate parenthesis — additional information inserted into a sentence that could be removed without changing the sentence's core meaning
Time to try this out: Insert a parenthetical phrase using paired brackets, e.g. 'The oldest building (built in 1642) stands in the town square'
When you adds a little extra detail inside a sentence — like "The teacher, who is very strict, gave us homework" — do they correctly use brackets, dashes, or commas around that added bit?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Brackets and dashes for parenthesis: Insert a parenthetical phrase using paired brackets, e.g. 'The oldest building (built in 1642) stands in the town square' Use paired dashes to add an aside or extra detail within a sentence, e.g. 'My brother — who is older than me — lives in London'
Let's discover the amazing world of Relative Clauses!
Form and use relative clauses beginning with relative pronouns (who, whose, whom, which, that) and relative adverbs (where, when, why) to add detail, qualify nouns, and create complex sentences
Time to try this out: Combine a main clause with a relative clause using 'who' or 'which' to add information about a noun, e.g. 'The dog, which had a red collar, barked loudly'

When you writes to add detail about a noun — like describing a friend "who loves football" or a place "where we always go on holiday" — do they build that into the same sentence using a relative clause?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Relative Clauses: Combine a main clause with a relative clause using 'who' or 'which' to add information about a noun, e.g. 'The dog, which had a red collar, barked loudly' Choose the correct relative pronoun (who for people, which for things, where for places, when for times) and identify the noun it refers back to
Let's explore Adjective Order in Sentences! Have you ever wondered about this?
Order adjectives within sentences according to conventional English patterns (opinion-size-age-shape-colour-origin-material-purpose) to produce natural-sounding descriptions

Let me show you something cool. Arrange multiple adjectives before a noun in conventional order, e.g. 'a lovely little old rectangular green French silver whittling knife' simplified to classroom examples like 'a small red bag' not 'a red small bag'
When you uses several describing words together — like "a tiny old wooden box" — does the order sound natural, or do they mix up the adjectives so it sounds odd?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Adjective Order in Sentences: Arrange multiple adjectives before a noun in conventional order, e.g. 'a lovely little old rectangular green French silver whittling knife' simplified to classroom examples like 'a small red bag' not 'a red small bag' Identify when adjective order sounds unnatural and rearrange to match English conventions, e.g. correct 'the wooden big table' to 'the big wooden table'
Today we're going to learn about Converting Words into Verbs. Get ready for an adventure!
Convert nouns and adjectives into verbs by adding suffixes -ate, -ise (-ize), and -ify, understanding how word class changes affect sentence construction
Let's practice together! Add -ate, -ise, or -ify to nouns or adjectives to form verbs, e.g. pollen → pollinate, advert → advertise, simple → simplify
Can you take a word like "active" or "sharp" and turn it into a verb by adding a suffix — knowing that "activate" and "sharpen" are the verb forms?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Converting Words into Verbs. Add -ate, -ise, or -ify to nouns or adjectives to form verbs, e.g. pollen → pollinate, advert → advertise, simple → simplify Choose the correct verb-forming suffix for a given root and use the resulting verb in a sentence, e.g. 'They needed to classify the animals' from 'class'
Have you ever thought about Verb Prefixes and Meaning? Let's find out together!
Use verb prefixes (dis-, de-, mis-, over-, re-) to change verb meaning, understanding how each prefix modifies the action expressed by the root verb

Let me show you something cool. Add verb prefixes to change meaning, e.g. dis- (disagree, disappear), de- (decompose, defrost), mis- (misunderstand, misbehave), over- (overlook, overreact), re- (redo, reconsider)
If you give you a verb like "appear" and ask them to use a prefix to change its meaning, can they produce words like "disappear", "reappear", or "misrepresent" and explain what the prefix does?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Verb Prefixes and Meaning: Add verb prefixes to change meaning, e.g. dis- (disagree, disappear), de- (decompose, defrost), mis- (misunderstand, misbehave), over- (overlook, overreact), re- (redo, reconsider) Select the appropriate verb prefix to express a specific meaning shift such as negation (dis-/mis-), reversal (de-/un-), repetition (re-), or excess (over-)
Today we're going to learn about Commas to avoid ambiguity. Get ready for an adventure!
Use commas to clarify meaning and avoid ambiguity in sentences where the absence of a comma could cause misreading
Let's practice together! Insert a comma to prevent ambiguity, e.g. 'Let's eat, Grandma' vs 'Let's eat Grandma' or 'Most of the time, travellers worry about their bags'
Can you explain why "Let's eat, Grandma" and "Let's eat Grandma" mean very different things — and use that kind of comma in their own writing to avoid confusion?

Great job! Today we learned about Commas to avoid ambiguity. Insert a comma to prevent ambiguity, e.g. 'Let's eat, Grandma' vs 'Let's eat Grandma' or 'Most of the time, travellers worry about their bags' Identify sentences where a missing comma changes the meaning and explain the two possible readings
Today we're going to learn about Commas Before Joining Words. Get ready for an adventure!
Use a comma before a coordinating conjunction (and, but, or, so, yet) when joining two independent clauses in a compound sentence
Let me show you something cool. Place a comma before the coordinating conjunction in a compound sentence, e.g. 'I wanted to go outside, but it was raining'
When you joins two complete thoughts with a word like "but" or "so" — like "I wanted to go, but it was raining" — do they put a comma before that joining word?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Commas Before Joining Words: Place a comma before the coordinating conjunction in a compound sentence, e.g. 'I wanted to go outside, but it was raining' Distinguish compound sentences (two independent clauses) from simple sentences with compound predicates, e.g. 'She sang and danced' needs no comma but 'She sang a song, and he played the piano' does
Let's discover the amazing world of Fixing Fragments & Run-Ons!
Recognise and correct sentence fragments (incomplete sentences lacking a subject or predicate) and run-on sentences (two or more independent clauses joined without proper punctuation or conjunctions)

Time to try this out: Identify a sentence fragment by checking whether it has both a subject and a predicate, e.g. recognise 'Running through the park' as a fragment and correct to 'The dog was running through the park'
If you's writing has a sentence like "Running down the street." or two ideas jammed together without any punctuation, can they spot that something's wrong and fix it?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Fixing Fragments & Run-Ons. Identify a sentence fragment by checking whether it has both a subject and a predicate, e.g. recognise 'Running through the park' as a fragment and correct to 'The dog was running through the park' Identify a run-on sentence where two independent clauses are fused without punctuation or a conjunction, e.g. 'I like cats I also like dogs' and correct using a full stop, comma + conjunction, or semicolon
